text: Alleluia. The word is repeated in some copies. (St. Jerome)<hr>But Hebrew has only one at the end of the preceding psalm: of which this appears to be a sequel. As in the former, God's favours were specified, so here the ingratitude of his people is acknowledged. (Calmet)<hr>It may refer to the Jews during, (Eusebius; Vatable) or after the captivity, (Calmet) and to all in the state of sin. (St. Jerome) (Berthier)<hr>The 47th and 48th verses agree with the three last, which were sung at the translation of the ark, 1 Paralipomenon 16:34. (Haydock)<hr>Hence that canticle now makes a part of our Psalm civ., xcv., and cv., with various alterations made by the prophet. (Berthier)<hr>Give glory. Literally, "confess" (Haydock) your sins, to obtain mercy, (St. Chrysostom) or rather praise God, (Theodoret) who is the source of all good. (Worthington)<hr>This was the usual prayer of the Israelites, in ceremonies of thanksgiving, 2 Paralipomenon 5:13. (Calmet)
Powers. See Ecclesiasticus 43:29. No one can sufficiently praise God's power or mercy. (Berthier)

Times. He only who perseveres to the end shall be crowned, Matthew 24:13., and Ezechiel xxxiii. (Haydock)<hr>Such as these can alone worthily sound forth God's praises, (Berthier) being so happy as to preserve their innocence, or at least to repent. (Worthington)

Us. Hebrew, "me." But Aquila, Syriac, etc., agree with the Septuagint. Some of the Jews were already returned, and pray for the liberation of their brethren. (Calmet)<hr>The Fathers explain it of Christian converts. (St. Chrysostom)<hr>People. According to thy gracious promises, that we may enjoy the blessings of thy elect. (Worthington)

Sinned. Here commences the confession of sins. The just man first accuses himself, Proverbs 18:17. (Calmet)<hr>The same prayer is repeated, Judith 7:19., and Daniel 9:5., as it might be applicable to the captives. (Berthier)<hr>We have injured ourselves, our neighbours, and our God. (Worthington)

Going up. Or proceeding. The banks of the sea were more elevated than the country from which the Israelites came, (Calmet) and the land of Chanaan was still higher. See Psalm 106:23., and Genesis 12:9. (Haydock)<hr>Sea even. Is not in the Septuagint. (Calmet)
Known. Isaias 63:12. He might justly (Worthington) have abandoned them to the Egyptians, Exodus 14:11.
Rebuked. Commanding with power, Nahum 1:4. (Calmet)<hr>Wilderness. Or plain country. (Haydock)

Redeemed. What price was given? This is a figure of baptism, in which the fruits of Christ's blood are applied to our souls. All sins are there destroyed, as the Egyptians were in the Sea. (St. Augustine, Ser. xlii. de Temp.) (Worthington)

Left. Manetho and Lorin assert, that Pharao reigned afterwards. But this is contrary to the Scripture. All who came to attack Israel perished. (Calmet)<hr>They supposed that the waters were enchanted. (St. Irenaeus 4:48.)
Words. And Moses his servant, Exodus 14:31., and 15:1.
Counsel. Three days after the passage of the sea, God laid injunctions upon them at Mara, which they would not observe, Exodus 15:22. (Calmet)<hr>They coveted unnecessary things. (Worthington)
Desire. Or they greedily sought for flesh meat, (Haydock) at Sin, or Pharan, Exodus 16:13., and Numbers 11:31. Chronological order is not observed. (Calmet)
Fulness. Or disgust: plesmone. Septuagint probably read zore, (as Numbers 11:20.) instead of razon. Hebrew, "leanness," (Calmet) which is a natural consequence of immoderate repletion. (Haydock)
Holy one. By his function (Worthington) of priest. Core wished to take his place, as the rest did to supplant Moses in the civil government, Numbers xvi. (Calmet)
Abiron. With Core, though his children were preserved. (Calmet)<hr>God presently resented the injury done to his ministers. (Eusebius)
Wicked. Two hundred and fifty accomplices, (Numbers 15:35.; Calmet) and fourteen thousand seven hundred murmurers. (Menochius)
Graven. Hebrew, "molten." The image must first be graven. (Berthier) (Exodus 32:3.)

Grass. This enhances their folly. God is often styled the glory of his people. (Calmet)<hr>Osee 10:5. contemptuously calls the golden calf, the glory of Samaria. (Haydock)<hr>This idolatry was truly blameable, (Romans 1:23.) and not the veneration paid by Catholics to the images of Christ, etc. (Worthington)<hr>Those who pretend that the Hebrews meant to adore the true God, and not the idol Apis, are here clearly refuted; they forgot God, etc., Acts 7:40. (Berthier)

Breach. Like a valiant captain, (Calmet) or pastor. (Haydock)<hr>God complains of those who neglected this duty, Ezechiel 13:5., and 22:30. The psalmist alluded to the conduct of Moses, after the people had adored the calf, (Exodus 32:10.) or when they had joined with Core, Numbers 16:44. (Calmet)<hr>The intercession of the saints is useful, (St. Augustine) still more after their victories. (St. Jerome ad Vig. ii.) (2 Machabees 15:14.) (Berthier)<hr>God spared the people for the sake of Moses. (Worthington)
Word. Which Moses, Josue, etc., announced, Numbers xiii., and 12:29. (Calmet)
Over them. Swearing (Calmet) to destroy them, (Haydock; Ezechiel 20:23.) and scatter them, as they now experienced, Leviticus 26:31., and Deuteronomy 28:36, 64.
Dead idols, without life, (Challoner) particularly Adonis (Calmet) whose death is bewailed by the poets, etc. (Haydock)<hr>Idols kill their votaries. (Worthington)

Multiplied. All who had murmured perished, or this may relate to the 24,000, Numbers 25:9. (Calmet)<hr>Ezechiel 20:23. St. Paul (1 Corinthians 10:8.) speaks of those 23,000 slain by the Levites. The Hebrew which has only 3,000 must be inaccurate. Temporal chastisements were intended to prefigure those which are eternal, as the Jews knew that such rewards and punishments were to be considered (Matthew 19:16.; Berthier) as the spiritual sense of the law. (Grotius, Jur. 2:20, 39.)

Evermore. Phinees thus by his pious zeal, merited (Worthington) God's favour, and the high priesthood (Numbers 25:13.; Calmet) for his posterity, for 900 years, though the latter promise seems to have been conditional. (Berthier)

He distinguished with his lips. Moses, by occasion of the people's rebellion and incredulity, was guilty of distinguishing with his lips; when, instead of speaking to the rock, as God had commanded, he said to the people, with a certain hesitation in his faith, Hear ye rebellious and incredulous: Can we from this rock bring out water for you? Numbers 20:10. (Challoner)<hr>For thus doubting of God's will, he was temporarily punished, Deuteronomy 1:37. (Worthington)<hr>The death of his sister, and the ingratitude and rebellion of the people disturbed him. (Theodoret) See Isaias 63:10. Houbigant follows the Arabic, "he delayed or hesitated." Yebatte means, "he spoke rashly." Some refer this, (Berthier) to God. "He reproached....that they," etc., (Menochius) which is not necessary, (Berthier) or he sentenced Moses to die. (Kimchi)

Them. Commanding no idolatrous government to be left in the land, nor any Chanaanite to be permitted to live, who should make opposition. The law of extermination must probably be thus restricted. The Jews neglected these ordinances, and hence became so often infected with idolatry, (Berthier) even to the end of the republic, when many still adored the sun. (Josephus, Jewish Wars 2:8.) (Exodus 23:32., and Josue 13:13.)

Chanaan. These unnatural sacrifices were perhaps practised under the judges, or at least under the kings. (Worthington)<hr>Some pretend that the children were only made to pass over or between fires. But this is refuted by facts, Leviticus 18:21., and 4 Kings 16:3., and Jeremias 7:30<hr>Polluted. Infecta: ephonoktanethe is used by Septuagint, in this sense, though it signifies also, "was slain;" interfecta, as most Latin Bibles and Fathers read, before the correction of Clement VIII. The former expression is more natural and conformable to the Hebrew. (Calmet)
And was. Hebrew, "and they were defiled." (St. Jerome)<hr>Inventions. Idols of their imaginations, (Haydock) and lusts. (Calmet)
Repented. Ceased to punish, (Eusebius) when the people repented. (St. Jerome)<hr>God then regarded them in mercy, having given them grace to repent. (Worthington)
Mercies. He inspired their enemies with pity for them. Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes shewed them favour, Psalm 83:12. (Calmet)

Save. These verses, which may be well recited in times of schism, (Worthington) occur, 1 Paralipomenon xvi., being probably inserted by Esdras, or some other prophet. But here they are in their proper place, as many of the Israelites are still in captivity. (Calmet)<hr>Yet as this is only a system, we may rather suppose, that David wrote this conclusion. He had to encounter various nations, who might have made captives; and he may beg that all may be united in the same faith, and avoid the society of the wicked. (Berthier)

So be it. The second Amen occurs not in Hebrew. But Alleluia is placed instead, which the Vulgate makes the title of the next psalm. (Haydock)<hr>This conclusion of the fourth book seems to be added by the collector, Psalm xl. (Calmet)<hr>Yet this is uncertain. (Haydock)
